title: Chapter 43: The Broadcast Test
content: *wren. day sixteen. amplification.*
The Signal Key woke her.

Not with sound—it made no sound. But Wren surfaced from shallow sleep with her hand already closed around it, fingers tight, palm sweating against the matte surface. The key was vibrating. Not a mechanical vibration; something deeper, a resonance that traveled through her skin and into the bone beneath, humming in concert with the data streams she'd been listening to for three days.

She sat up. The sleeping bag tangled around her legs. The data center was dark except for the router LEDs, green and steady, and the faint amber glow of the city through the high ventilation windows. Two AM. She didn't need to check; she could feel it in the rhythm of the traffic outside—the night shift patterns, the reduced bandwidth, the slow pulse of a city that never fully slept.

The key was resonating with something. She held it up to the faint light and turned it over. Still matte black. Still featureless. But warmer than before—almost hot now, like a stone left in the sun. And the resonance was getting stronger.
She understood. It was responding to her.

The glitch-power had been growing all week. Her capacity to hear the data streams, to manipulate signals, to push against the city's algorithmic immune system—every day it expanded. And the Signal Key, dormant since Ash had stolen it, was designed to respond to exactly this kind of signal. It was waiting for amplitude. It was waiting for her to be loud enough to activate it.

Wren stood. She was shaking—the kind of full-body tremor that came after pushing too hard, listening too long. But the key was buzzing in her hand now, and the buzz was insistent, and she was done waiting.

She walked to the monitors. Three screens, still showing the NeuroScale logo, connected to emergency power and, through that, to the building's network—connected, by extension, to the city's grid. Not directly. Not efficiently. But connected.
She placed the Signal Key on the console desk, flat on its face, and pressed both palms against it.
The effect was immediate.

It wasn't amplification. It was multiplication. The glitch-power that lived in her nervous system flowed into the key and came back transformed—a hundred times louder, a hundred times denser, a hundred times more. She gasped. The sensation was like shouting into a canyon and hearing a stadium roar back. Her signal, her capacity, her single fragile voice—expanded until it filled the room and then the building and then the block.

The monitors went white. All three, simultaneously—not the slow pixel-by-pixel she'd managed before but an instant whiteout, every phosphor firing at once. The fluorescent tube overhead, dead for two days, crackled and flickered back to life. The router LEDs blazed green, all of them, maximum throughput. The data center came alive around her like a machine remembering it had a purpose.

She pulled back. The whiteout held for two seconds, then the monitors snapped to black. The tube died again. The routers settled. She was on her knees—she didn't remember falling—and her nose was bleeding freely, both nostrils, blood pattering on the floor tiles like rain.
One hundred times. The key amplified her signal by a factor of one hundred.

She sat on the cold floor and breathed. The copper taste filled her mouth. Her hands were shaking so badly she had to sit on them to still the tremor. But underneath the exhaustion and the pain and the nosebleed, something else burned: elation. Sharp and bright and dangerous.
One hundred times.

Her best unassisted push had held text for five seconds on a closed network before auto-correct reclaimed it. Amplified by a hundred, that was... theoretically over eight minutes. But theory didn't account for the auto-correct scaling—on a public network, the correction algorithms would attack faster and harder, deploying countermeasures proportional to the severity of the breach. She wouldn't get eight minutes on a public network. She might get seconds.

But seconds might be enough. If the signal was loud enough, bright enough, undeniable enough—if it hit every screen in a wide radius simultaneously—then even a few seconds could plant a seed. A seed that witnesses would remember. A seed that couldn't be entirely erased because it had lived in human minds before the algorithms scrubbed it from the machines.
She needed to test it. Tonight.

Wren wiped her face and stood. She went to the tablet and opened the subject files. Thirteen folders. She chose Subject 07: Petra Volkov. If this worked—when this worked—she wanted Petra to be first. The woman who had trusted NeuroScale with her anxiety and been repaid with erasure. The woman whose name deserved to be spoken.

She copied Petra's file to a separate directory: name, photograph, trial dates, termination date, and one word in red block letters—TERMINATED. She prepared it as a broadcast packet, structured for display, optimized for maximum visibility. Then she waited for dawn.

The morning came gray and slow. Wren positioned herself at the console with the Signal Key pressed between her palms. The data center's network connection was limited—decommissioned infrastructure, minimal bandwidth—but it was enough. From this node, she could reach the local grid. Two blocks. Maybe three. Enough for a proof of concept.

She listened first. The data streams outside were in their morning configuration—dense traffic, corporate networks booting up, consumer feeds cycling through breakfast content. The auto-correct algorithms were at full strength, vigilant, scrubbing any content that didn't match authorized parameters. This was the hardest possible time to push unauthorized data.
Good. If it worked now, it would work anytime.

She fed Petra's file into the signal. Name. Photograph. Dates. That word—TERMINATED. She held the image clearly in her mind, felt it translate into frequency, felt the glitch-power wrap around it and compress it into a dense digital payload. Then she pushed the payload into the Signal Key.

The amplification hit like a physical force. The key blazed hot against her palms—she smelled burning skin, her own skin, and bit down on the pain. The payload expanded, multiplied, exploded outward through the data center's network connection and into the city's grid.
Every screen in a two-block radius went white.

Wren saw it through the data streams—she couldn't see the street from here, but she could feel the displays responding. The coffee shop on the corner. The transit display at the bus stop. The medical office waiting room. The real estate window. The bank of monitors in the police precinct lobby. Every connected screen in range, simultaneously overwhelmed by the amplified signal, displaying the same image:

Petra Volkov. Brunette, mid-thirties, kind face, direct gaze. Her name in bold text above the photograph. Her trial dates below. And across the bottom, in red that blazed like a wound: **TERMINATED**.

The auto-correct responded in three seconds. Wren felt it—countermeasures deploying, code surging through the grid, an algorithmic immune system rushing to quarantine the breach. She pushed harder. The key burned. The image held on the coffee shop screen, on the transit display, on the medical office monitor, while the algorithms battered at it from every direction.
Five seconds. Six. Seven.

She heard something new—exclamation points in the data stream. Humans noticing. A barista pointing. A commuter stopping mid-stride. A receptionist reaching for her phone. People were seeing it. Living witnesses, biological memory, brains that the auto-correct couldn't scrub.
Eight seconds. Nine.

The first screens started to fall. The transit display snapped to a route map. The real estate window returned to property listings. The countermeasures were learning, adapting, finding the specific frequency of her amplified signal and damping it.
Ten seconds.

All screens restored. The breach sealed. Normal content flowed back into every display as if nothing had happened. The auto-correct algorithms settled into surveillance mode, scanning for residual contamination, finding none.

It was over. Ten seconds of visibility across a two-block radius. Ten seconds of Petra Volkov's face and name and the word that described what NeuroScale had done to her. Ten seconds that the algorithms had erased but that a dozen human minds still held.

Wren released the Signal Key. It clattered on the desk. Her palms were red and blistered—second-degree burns in the shape of the key's rectangular outline. The pain hit her in a delayed wave, and she had to grip the desk edge to stay upright. Blood from her nose dripped onto the console. Her vision doubled, then steadied.

She looked at her burned hands. She looked at the tablet, where Petra's file was still open. She looked at the Signal Key, already cooling on the desk, its surface matte and unmarked as if nothing had happened.
Ten seconds. Two blocks. That was the proof of concept.

But if she could reach NeuroScale's core server—the central hub that connected every display in the city, every network, every feed—if she could push the Signal Key's amplified signal through that conduit—the reach wouldn't be two blocks. It would be everywhere. Every screen. Every device. Every connected surface in the city, and through the city's networks, every city connected to the same infrastructure.
Everything. She could broadcast to everything.
But first she had to get inside Blackmoss. She had to reach NeuroScale's core server. She had to survive long enough to push the button.

Wren ran cold water over her burned palms. The pain was exquisite—clear and present and impossible to ignore. She welcomed it. Pain meant she was still here. Still functional. Still capable of burning herself to ash for ten seconds of truth.

Two days left. Two days until ANX-00 launched and the erasure went public. Two days to plan an infiltration, rescue Ash, and broadcast the files from inside the most secure facility on the eastern seaboard.

She dried her hands carefully. She wrapped them in gauze from the first-aid kit she'd found in the janitor's closet. She sat down at the console and opened the tablet and began designing the broadcast packet for all thirteen subjects. Not just Petra now. All of them. Every name, every face, every date, every termination. Thirteen lives, amplified through the Signal Key, pushed through NeuroScale's own infrastructure, visible to everyone.

The algorithms would try to erase it. They would deploy every countermeasure, every filter, every scrubbing protocol. But for a few seconds—maybe ten, maybe more if the core server's bandwidth sustained the amplification—the truth would be visible. Undeniable. Seen.
And what people saw, they remembered. The algorithms could erase screens. They couldn't erase minds.
The data streams hummed. The router LEDs blinked. Somewhere in Blackmoss, Ash's implant sent its eleven-minute ping, and Wren heard it like a heartbeat—the only heartbeat that mattered.
Two days. She had two days, and she was going to make them count.
